item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is good, I don't know much about the surrounding lore, but I know enough for this to be entertaining!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you very much! I appreciate the translation, and the preservation of the pronouns.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Translated to Russian. «Ve»/«ver» preserved as it was possible in our language.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I selected ve/ver intentionally.

Hespera is the leader of the Cogwork Orthodoxy, in this canon. As such, ve is also a high-ranking member of the Unified Church of the Broken God. The Foundation absolutely would be aware of ver, and ver gender identity.

Outside of that, in my headcanon, the Foundation cares a great deal about accuracy, and would use someone's pronouns correctly despite what people in our universe might think about them. As an individual, I accept and will utilize people's correct pronouns because that's the right thing to do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Does it matter if the Foundation knows or not?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
But how would the Foundation know that? Unless Hespera told them (which seems not to be the case), what reason would they have to select that pronoun set for the transcript?

(Or they just select pronouns at random, which kind of defeats the purpose of having specific pronoun sets in the first place…)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ve/ver is a set of neopronouns, and I assume it's because that's what Malyce chose to use for ver.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this its very well done but i have one problem

Hespera why is she called "ver" i don't get it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is my head canon for Bumaro from now on :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is interesting to read for me, as I'm not as familiar with the surrounding lore as I should be; however, it still works very well for me with that in mind. I agree with ManyMeats that the description is a tad dry, and I felt it took a little too long to get there, but once it did I was totally on board. Your writing is some of the most immersive on the site for me. Particularly the last log was very effective, the build-up to it set the groundwork excellently, and that final log was full of very satisfying and engaging payoff. "This is very bad." gave me a pang of fear in a really great way that I've missed from many articles. Easy +1. I look forward to the rest of this canon, it's engrossing in a very unique and compelling way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I hate this, but it was certainly done well. As usual, excellent performance MG, you captured the plight that the Broken Church would surely be in if Bumaro were to be taken down. The Mekhanics angle was a nice touch as well, after all your Sarks would need all the help they could get to take down the man behind the CotBG. ;)

+1, my friend

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Per this: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Parts-per_notation

1 of → per per per per per per
= ⭨ cent 1,000 10,000 100,000 million billion
of ↓   (%) (‰) (‱) (pcm) (ppm) (ppb)
% 1 0.1 0.01 0.001 0.0001 10−7
‰ 10 1 0.1 0.01 0.001 10−6
‱ 100 10 1 0.1 0.01 10−5
pcm 1,000 100 10 1 0.1 0.0001
ppm 10,000 1,000 100 10 1 0.001
ppb 107 106 105 10,000 1,000 1

‰ and ppm exist in the same continuum as proper notation.

It irritates me too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While I am happy to see the ophidian girl on the mainlist finally, you also managed to make me sad for the CotBG. So I'm going to call this a successful article on both fronts.

One nitpick: although it's not incorrect, using both ‰ and ppm looks weird, and I would suggest you figure out a more consistent unit to use.

Edit:

wrapped in several layers of purple funerial velvet

Please tell me this is aniline purple and not, like, Murex…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This tickles my lore-bits so hard. The thematic unity here is *so strong*, it's clear to anyone who reads more than one of these pieces how deeply and thoroughly you've 'mastered' the universe you're setting this in.

As I expressed privately to you, some of the Description probably borders on "too dry" but the rest of the article more than makes up for it several times over (for me). Just like our boy Bumaro, I've gushed enough, this is a big +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First of all: +1

It might not be completely a style/theme I prefer to read, but I have to admit it was enjoyable and a strong piece nonetheless. Good job, Malyce.

The article has very cohesive and detailed writing. The terms and descriptions used are definitely from the right field-terminologies. The only term I didn't like though was salinity, because I have never seen that used before. Perhaps that might be an extra reason to actually use it, or I might just be "too salty".

The sections are very clean, whilst the content definitely has depth and intrigue. The best example would be the object dossier (in my opinion), although the memo is great too. The transcript is great too, but I had to re-read some stuff because the grey colour wasn't super easy on my eyes so I can't 100% say this section is as fluent as the rest. It definitely is still as intriguing though. (This is not your fault, sometimes stuff like this happens when I read whilst being tired.)

I will definitely read this again if I stumble upon the hub again sometime.

P.S. In the footbar you have SCP-2833- Memoria Adytum Hub - SCP-2835, Shouldn't it say SCP-2834 here?

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
Hubris is interesting, because you get people who are often very clever, very powerful, have achieved great things, and then something goes wrong - they just don't know when to stop.
- Margaret McMillan

And thus another icon falls, and the world begins its downward spiral into chaos.

A huge thank you to those that helped me to make this better: ManyMeats, Doctor Fullham, plaidypus, DrBleep, & Grigori Karpin
